---
title: "Welcome to EVE 113 (Coastal Marine Microbes)!"
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(rmarkdown)
```

![Abalone in a Kelp Forest \| Photo Credit: Stefan Andrews / Ocean Image Bank](images/clipboard-1943924653.png)

Welcome! Let's get excited about coastal marine microbial ecology! This "course" was written and developed with the intrepid, undergraduate ecologist in mind. It makes use of real-world ecological questions using real-world ecological data. These tutorials were also designed to meet a variety of experience levels for students enrolled in EVE 113.

Some of you may have zero experience using R; Others may be well versed in using the console, tidyverse and ggplot, but may be unfamiliar with things like vegan or `unix`. It's completely possible this is your first time ever hearing of R (but hopefully not the last!), or it could be your hundredth. Either way, no matter the situation, **this resource is meant to serve you!** If there is a problem you're hoping to tackle that is not included in this guide, please don't hesitate to notify your TA or instructor. We are here to help you succeed and grow your toolkit as an ecologist.

This guide is setup like a website: We have a home page (the page you're on now), the Getting Started page, and the Tutorial page(s). You can navigate between each of these by simply heading to the top of the web browser you're currently viewing this on and clicking on the tabs. This home page won't contain more than a general overview of how the tutorials are structured, how to get the most out of your learning, and information for further "R education" beyond EVE 113.

::: callout-important
**Work associated with Tutorial 1 is [due August 27th]{.underline}**, prior to our first data workshop day. It is our expectation that you understand the learning objectives in each tutorial prior to coming to class on the 27th.
:::

# What is R?

R is an open source scripted programming language used for statistical analyses and graphical visualization of data. Because R is an open source platform, it means that independent developers, researchers, and scientists alike have helped contribute to additional packages (or "add-ons") that further extend the programs functionality. R is an extraordinarily valuable tool and it is used (nearly) ubiquitously across a plethora of disciplines. If you have any interest in continuing work in ecology, evolution, or the environmental/biological sciences, you will most certainty encounter R again at some point.

In this course, we will be coding in R using the **free** software interface application known as **RStudio**. RStudio is a user-friendly IDE (integrated development environment) where you can access both the Console and the Terminal (we will talk about what both of those means later). R and RStudio work with any operating system you have on hand, whether that be Windows, Apple (macOS), or Linux. (Although, if you're operating on Linux, I might just assume you already have R downloaded in some capacity).

# Course Design

Overall, our class learning objectives for data analysis this summer are (broadly) as follows:

1.  Introduce students to the R programming language - specifically with ecological and biological data in mind.

2.  Introduce and learn how to code in the tidyverse (and all of its amazing sub-packages).

3.  Learn how to make plots and conduct introductory statistical analyses.

4.  Learn what the dada2 pipeline is and how it is applied to data you (the student) has collected.

5.  Get excited about Coastal Marine Microbial Ecology!

::: callout-note
***Your learning here will be a product of the amount of time you spend engaging with the material, practicing your skills, and asking questions when they arise.***

You should always return to this website if you need to revisit any tutorials from earlier in the term (e.g., you're doing an analysis but forgot the code to run said analysis, you can return to this website and navigate to the top right corner search bar to find what you're looking for). **I am always available via email or in office hours to help you when you get stuck. [Please come to me before finding your way to ask questions to chatGPT or AI.]{.underline}**

I want to see you succeed!! I believe you are so much smarter and more capable than AI. AND I believe the best way you will learn more is by asking a real human with real-world experience rather than AI (plus it's literally my job to help you when you get stuck)!
:::

I've tried to make this experience as learner-friendly as possible. This website is struictured around a series of tutorials containing practice problems and examples that will prepare you for the work we will be doing in class (and work you will do beyond EVE 113).

Your **tutorials** are where you will *gain skills and knowledge* to satisfy our **learning objectives**.

The **DIYs** are ways you can *test or practice your new skills or knowledge* as you go.

The **Questions** (only in Tutorial 1) are similar to the DIYs, but form the component parts of the **R Preparedness assignment**.

Again: [**The R Preparedness assignment associated with Tutorial 1 content will be due August 27th.**]{.underline} Give yourself ample time to work through this! You will thank me later.

## Learning Objectives

At the top of every tutorial, there will be numbered **learning objectives** stating the goals and material covered in the tutorial itself. They will look like:

1.  A series of bullet pointed or objectives for that specific tutorial.

2.  There might be more than one learning objective, or sub-learning objectives.

3.  This numbered list will give you a sneak peek into what the tutorial will be covering.

4.  If you already know how to do some things in the learning objectives then you can consider the tutorial a good chance to review your knowledge.

## The *Actual* Tutorial

The tutorials will give you the skills needed to complete your final project/paper. I will try to include screenshots of an actual working RStudio environment where applicable so you can better understand what we're supposed to be doing. However, most tutorials will largely be made up of "chunks" of code that I expect you to either directly copy/paste into your RStudio or, better yet, type yourself.

::: callout-tip
***Actually typing the code yourself, rather than copy/pasting will help you better absorb the material and skills you are learning.***
:::

Code chunks included in tutorials will looks like this:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
ggplot2::ggplot(data = cars, aes(x = speed, y = dist)) +
  geom_point() 
```

Or maybe even like this:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
increment1 <- function(input){
  incremented_value <- input + 1
  return(incremented_value)
}
```

## Do It Yourself and Questions

My **Do It Yourself (or DIYs)** are built-in to provide short breaks between content-heavy topics. These allow you to test and implement the knowledge you gain as you move along in the tutorials. It's also a great way to see or self-test if you understand what we're doing.

**There are no DIYs in Tutorial 1, instead there are only Questions**, which are the component parts to your R Preparedness assignment. This will help me evaluate if you've actually completed the learning objectives in the tutorials and are ready to tackle working with our class data.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**Do It Yourself T0-00**

This is what a DIY will look like. It will include the header above with a number associated with that tutorial. Say, for example, this is DIY 7 in Tutorial 4, the header would look like:

**Do It Yourself T4-07**

a\. Then each DIY may include multiple parts.

b\. Each part will be a specific task or question that may ask you to explain a topic, write a line of code, or complete a task.
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**Question 0**

Questions look exactly like DIYs, but are only found in Tutorial 1. They are meant to be answered like DIYs, but unlike DIYs, there will be no answer key for them located in Tutorial 1. The same questions are copied into your R-Prep.rmd for easy reference as you complete your work.
:::

By August 27th you will turn in your R-Prep.rmd to your TA (me) for feedback. Don't feel pressured to get "everything correct" but rather I'd like you to focus on the actual acquisition of knowledge.

### Answer Keys

DIY answer keys will be provided at the end of every tutorial that includes the DIYs. They will look like the following:

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**DIY T0-00**

0a. The answer to a provided theoretical question.

0b.

```{r}
# the code answer to a question, if asked to write a line of code
```
:::

### Completing Your R Preparedness Assignment

Navigate to Files on our class Canvas page and find the [R Preparedness folder](https://canvas.ucdavis.edu/courses/1122338/files/folder/R%20Preparedness). Download the .rmd file named "R-Prep". At the end of each tutorial, you will knit your .rmd document as an html and submit it via canvas by the due date. More on how to do this in Tutorial 0.

The .rmd will include chunks of code and places for you to answer the questions as you work through Tutorial 1.

## Review

At the end of every tutorial, there will be a review (and celebration) of all that you've accomplished.

1.  If you read the review and can mentally say: "Yes! I know how to answer all of these questions" than congratulations, you've completed all the learning objectives!!
2.  You should give yourself a massive pat on the back and do a little celebration when you can do that (it's a big deal)!
3.  If you find yourself unable to understand a topic in the review, I suggest taking time to review the tutorial again to make sure you are understanding what is expected of you.

I will end every tutorial with a message and a "celebration" photo, and that is how you know you've reached the end of the educational content. ***Hooray!!!***

![](images/clipboard-168018562.png){width="199"}

## Works Cited

At very, very bottom, I will cite any of the packages or resources I used to make the tutorial. When you're writing up your final report, you may refer back to these tutorials for the citation information to include in your own works cited. **Please make sure you're citing the necessary software you're using in your work (giving credit where credit is due is very important to avoid plagiarism and promote reproducibility)!**

# Why You Should Continue Learning R Beyond This Course...

[Auker and Barthelmess, 2020](https://esajournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ecs2.3060) synthesize how important learning how to use R has become for Ecologists and Biologists just within the past decade. Relatively any job in this field nowadays likely requires some fundamental (or, occasionally, even advanced) knowledge of how to use R as a tool to analyze ecological data.

It's true that learning a new programming language can, in simple terms, suck. However, being able to leverage these tools in your learning can be an invaluable skill both short- and long-term.

I know in the age of Artificial Intelligence, learning something like this may seem like a fruitless task, but evidence has shown that AI actually isn't all that great at helping you write code to analyze data ([Becker et al., 2026](https://metr.org/blog/2026-02-24-uplift-update/#wider-adoption-of-ai-has-made-it-more-difficult-to-measure-task-level-productivity)). These researchers found that AI, in it's current state as of February 2026, is roughly 20% slower than actual researchers at completing a coding or development task. At the task level, there may be some marginal benefits to utilizing a LLM like ChatGPT or Gemini to solve short-term code bugs, but not in the grand scheme of an entire project. You, the student scientist, are likely better at handling, parsing, and analyzing your own data – no matter how much a beginner you may think you are.

**Therefore, I implore you to give learning R a honest try before turning to AI to complete your assignments for you.** Not only will you gain more hands-on understanding of the material, but you may also gain a renewed appreciation for the step-by-step process of working with your very own data for the first time. I am also happy to be a resource, if needed, to help troubleshoot. Things don't always work as we intend them to; We may mistype a character here or there, possibly use the wrong command for something it's not meant to actually do. These can be valuable parts of the learning experience, so best to let them happen and work through them before letting an AI assistant feed you an "out-of-the-box" solution that may or may not aid in your learning.

If you're excited about the prospect of learning more in R, there are a plethora of resources that exist out there that can teach you! I'd recommend the following places, to start:

- [R for Data Science by Wickham and Grolemund](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/)

- [R for the rest of us](https://rfortherestofus.com/)

- [Getting Started with R: An Introduction for Biologists by Beckerman and Petchy](https://www.davidzeleny.net/wiki/lib/exe/fetch.php/recol:materials:beckerman-petchey2012_getting-started-with-r-an-introduction-for-biologists2.pdf)

- [R Coding for Ecology by Rocchini](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-031-99665-8)

If you find none of these fit your style, or don't fit your learning needs, lets sit down and chat to find something better for you.

## Answer Key

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple" icon="false"}
**DIY T0-00**

0a. (Again) this is what DIY answer keys will look like.

0b.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
# the code answer to a question, if asked to write a line of code
exmaple code here blah blah blah
```
:::
